summary: Gruber reviews the 2016 Touch Bar MacBook Pros while placing them in the broader context of Apple's evolving laptop lineup philosophy. He traces how the MacBook Air disrupted Apple's simple consumer/pro matrix by introducing 'niceness' — thinness and lightness — as a value proposition distinct from raw performance. He argues the Air and MacBook names have essentially swapped roles since 2008, and that 'Air' as a concept is now meaningless because everything Apple makes prioritizes thinness. The new MacBook Pros continue this trajectory: 'pro' now means nicer, not just faster. Despite legitimate gripes about dongles, missing MagSafe, and RAM limits, Gruber sees the Touch Bar as evidence Apple is investing seriously in the Mac's future rather than sunsetting it.
key_points: The MacBook Air's original value proposition (paying a premium for thinness over performance) has flipped — the Air is now the budget option and the no-adjective MacBook occupies the Air's old premium-thin niche
Apple's definition of 'pro' has shifted from raw performance to overall niceness: build quality, design refinement, and premium feel
The Touch Bar is a costly, carefully engineered embedded iOS device that refutes the narrative that Apple is abandoning the Mac
The new MacBook Pros mirror the original Air's reception — griping about missing ports and dongles now, but designed for the near future
Apple's nickel-and-diming on power cords and USB-C to Lightning cables is indefensible on machines costing thousands of dollars
money_quotes: What Apple means by 'pro' is tied very much to being nicer.
Everything Apple makes today is an Air model in spirit. The name 'Air' is no longer meaningful.
MacBook Air buyers were paying a premium not for performance (or to use the old word, power) but instead for niceness.
It took about three days using Touch ID on this review unit before I instinctively tried to use it to unlock my old MacBook. Typing my login password now feels archaic.

It is almost criminal that the extended power cord is now a $19 peripheral, and not included in the box. These machines cost upwards of $4,000 and they're going to nickel-and-dime you over a power cord?

I'd be more worried about Apple's commitment to the Mac if they did the easy thing — easier both technically and in terms of initial critical response — and just stuck a retina display in a MacBook Air and called it a day.
No tablet will ever be as nice as a notebook as these MacBook Pros.
MagSafe sure was a great idea, and it will be missed.
key_insight: Apple's redefinition of 'pro' from raw performance to overall niceness and design refinement explains the new MacBook Pros' tradeoffs and signals genuine long-term investment in the Mac.
